summary: Gruber argues that Alan Dye's departure from Apple to Meta as chief design officer is the best personnel news Apple has had in decades. Dye, who came from a brand and print advertising background rather than interaction design, led Apple's software design team for a decade that Gruber characterizes as a disaster — prioritizing how things look over how they work. His replacement, longtime Apple designer Stephen Lemay, is a career interface/interaction designer who is universally respected within Apple's ranks. Gruber theorizes the directional shift may be happenstance — Lemay was chosen for loyalty and political safety after Apple was blindsided by Dye's departure, rather than because leadership recognized the need for change. Nevertheless, Gruber believes this could be the best thing to happen to Apple's HI design since Steve Jobs's passing.
key_points: Alan Dye's background was in brand/fashion design (Kate Spade, Ogilvy), not interaction design — a fundamental mismatch that Jony Ive himself now apparently recognizes as his biggest mistake
Stephen Lemay's selection may have been driven by loyalty concerns after Dye's surprise departure rather than a deliberate directional shift, since other Dye allies were also poached by Meta

There is near-universal agreement among UI design practitioners that Dye's tenure was a failure, with dozens of talented designers leaving Apple out of frustration to work at places like LoveFrom and OpenAI
The Liquid Glass 'clear/tinted' preference setting was effectively an admission that Dye's design philosophy — prioritizing appearance over usability — had failed
Dye's real talent was political, not design: his position seemed unassailable despite widespread internal and external criticism, and it took his own decision to leave rather than being pushed out
money_quotes: Alan Dye doesn't actually care about design.

It's rather extraordinary in today's hyper-partisan world that there's nearly universal agreement amongst actual practitioners of user-interface design that Alan Dye is a fraud who led the company deeply astray.

Alan Dye seems like exactly the sort of person Jobs was describing in the first part of that quote: 'People think it's this veneer — that the designers are handed this box and told, Make it look good!'
I swear I had conversations in which I mentioned 'key window' and no one knew what I meant.
Dye was an anchor holding Apple back, but might elevate design at Meta.
I don't think Alan Dye could get a job as a barista at LoveFrom.
The average IQ of both companies has increased.
His political skill was so profound that it was his decision to leave, despite the fact that his tenure is considered a disaster by actual designers inside and outside the company.
key_insight: Alan Dye's decade leading Apple's software design was a mismatch of brand thinking applied to interaction design, and his departure — however accidental — opens the door for Apple to return to its tradition of design that prioritizes how things work, not just how they look.
